title: Visitor Safety Induction Missing Key Elements
scene_description: At the reception desk, you review the visitor safety induction log. Visitors receive a short pamphlet and sign in. However, the induction doesn't cover emergency evacuation routes specific to chemical areas, and there's no mention of the types of hazards present in each zone. The receptionist says 'we tell them to follow their escort and not touch anything.'
explanation: This is a non-compliance under Training & Competency. Under ISHL Article 59 and the related Ordinance on Industrial Safety and Health, employers must provide safety and health education that covers the hazards of the workplace. While visitors aren't employees, ISHL Article 25 places a duty on the employer to prevent danger to all persons present. A visitor induction that omits chemical hazard awareness and specific evacuation procedures for a chemical lab fails this duty.
source_reference: ISHL Article 25, Article 59, Ordinance on Industrial Safety and Health Article 35

title: Properly Posted PPE Changing Protocol
scene_description: In the changing area, a clearly formatted sign — in both Japanese and English — details the PPE donning and doffing sequence for each lab zone. Clean lab coats hang on numbered hooks. A mirror is positioned at the exit for self-inspection. Used PPE goes into a labeled hamper. The posted protocol matches the ISHL requirements for the substances used in the facility.
explanation: This is compliant. ISHL Article 22 and the Ordinance on Prevention of Hazards Due to Specified Chemical Substances require appropriate PPE for chemical handling. The changing area demonstrates best practice: clear protocols, proper organization, bilingual signage, and a system for separating clean and contaminated PPE. The posted sequence aligns with the substances used in the facility.
source_reference: ISHL Article 22, Ordinance on Prevention of Hazards Due to Specified Chemical Substances Article 38

title: Corridor Emergency Signage Using Wrong Color Coding
scene_description: Along the main corridor, you notice emergency and hazard signs posted at regular intervals. However, several signs use color coding that doesn't match the Japanese Industrial Standards. A 'Deleterious Substance' storage sign uses white text on a blue background instead of the prescribed red text on white background. Nearby, a 'Poison' warning sign uses black text on yellow instead of white text on red.
explanation: This is a non-compliance under Permits, Signage & Documentation. Japan's Poisonous and Deleterious Substances Control Act (毒物及び劇物取締法) prescribes specific signage: poisonous substances require white text on a red background (白地に赤文字 is reversed — 赤地に白文字), and deleterious substances require red text on a white background (白地に赤文字). These color requirements are legally mandated and not interchangeable with international conventions. Reversing or substituting colors constitutes non-compliance.
source_reference: Poisonous and Deleterious Substances Control Act Article 12, Enforcement Regulations Article 11-5

title: Safety & Health Committee Meeting Minutes — Frequency Question
scene_description: On the corridor notice board, you find the minutes of the Safety and Health Committee (安全衛生委員会) meetings. The most recent meeting was five weeks ago. Topics covered include review of Work Environment Measurement results and follow-up on corrective actions. Worker representatives and management signatures are present. The committee secretary explains the lab director was traveling last month so they 'combined' that meeting with the upcoming one — meaning one month was skipped.
explanation: This situation is ambiguous. ISHL Article 19 requires the Safety and Health Committee to meet at least once per month. A five-week gap technically means one calendar month was missed. However, the committee is otherwise functional — minutes are substantive, representation is balanced, and corrective actions are tracked. An auditor could reasonably cite the missed monthly meeting as a technical non-compliance under ISHL, or could note it as an observation with a recommendation to ensure monthly cadence is maintained regardless of scheduling conflicts. The 'combining' of meetings suggests the intent exists but the execution fell short.
source_reference: ISHL Articles 17, 18, 19; Ordinance on Industrial Safety and Health Article 23

title: Work Environment Measurement Missing B-Measurements
scene_description: In the analytical lab, you review the most recent Work Environment Measurement (作業環境測定) records. The report shows A-measurements were taken at a proper grid pattern across the room. However, the B-measurement column — personal exposure readings at the position of highest expected concentration near the fume hoods — is blank. The lab manager explains 'we always do the room grid measurements, the personal ones seemed redundant since the A-measurements were all within limits.'
explanation: This is a non-compliance under Monitoring & Health Surveillance. Japan's Work Environment Measurement Standards (作業環境測定基準) require both A-measurements (area sampling on a systematic grid) AND B-measurements (sampling at locations of highest expected exposure) to complete a valid assessment. Without B-measurements, the Management Classification cannot be properly determined, regardless of A-measurement results. The assessment is incomplete and the work environment cannot be legally classified.
source_reference: ISHL Article 65, Work Environment Measurement Act, Work Environment Measurement Standards Article 2, Article 10

title: HPLC Solvent Waste Segregation Ambiguity
scene_description: Near the HPLC instruments, you notice two waste containers: one labeled 'Halogenated Solvents' and one labeled 'Non-Halogenated Solvents.' However, one analyst is using a mixed-solvent gradient method (acetonitrile/water with trifluoroacetic acid). She places the waste in the halogenated container. Another analyst running a similar method puts his waste in the non-halogenated container. When asked, both say they were trained but disagree on how TFA-containing waste should be classified.
explanation: This situation is ambiguous. TFA (trifluoroacetic acid) is technically a halogenated compound, so placing the waste in the halogenated container is chemically defensible. However, the inconsistency between analysts indicates the SOP either doesn't address this edge case or wasn't effectively communicated. Under ISHL's risk assessment obligation and the Waste Management Act, the key issue is whether the facility has a documented protocol that addresses mixed-solvent waste classification. Without seeing the SOP, an auditor could reasonably flag this as a training gap or accept it as a borderline case with a recommendation to clarify procedures.
source_reference: ISHL Article 57-3, Waste Management and Public Cleansing Act, PRTR Act

title: Glovebox Nitrogen Purge System Operating Correctly
scene_description: In the synthesis lab, a researcher is working with air-sensitive reagents inside a glovebox. The oxygen and moisture monitors both show readings below 1 ppm. The nitrogen purge system is functioning — you can see the positive pressure reading. The daily calibration log for the monitors is current. Antechamber cycles are logged. The glovebox gloves were inspected and replaced within the manufacturer's recommended interval.
explanation: This is compliant. The glovebox demonstrates proper engineering controls as required by the Ordinance on Prevention of Hazards Due to Specified Chemical Substances. The atmosphere is controlled and monitored, calibration records are maintained, and the equipment is inspected according to schedule. Under ISHL Article 20, employers must take measures to prevent dangers from machinery — the glovebox maintenance and monitoring program satisfies this requirement.
source_reference: ISHL Article 20, Ordinance on Prevention of Hazards Due to Specified Chemical Substances

title: Health Examination Using Wrong Biomarker
scene_description: You review the special health examination records for researchers working with organic solvents. The facility conducts examinations every six months as required. However, for workers exposed to toluene, the lab is measuring urinary hippuric acid as the biological exposure index. Recent ISHL amendments updated the required biomarker for toluene exposure monitoring — hippuric acid is no longer the prescribed indicator.
explanation: This is a non-compliance under Monitoring & Health Surveillance. ISHL Article 66 and the Ordinance on Prevention of Organic Solvent Poisoning require substance-specific health examinations using prescribed biomarkers. Japan updated the required biological monitoring parameters — for toluene, urinary hippuric acid was replaced with urinary o-cresol as the specified biomarker. Conducting the examination using the outdated biomarker means the health examination does not satisfy the legal requirement, even though examinations are being performed at the correct frequency.
source_reference: ISHL Article 66, Ordinance on Prevention of Organic Solvent Poisoning Article 29, Special Health Examination Guidelines

title: Poisonous Substance Storage Sign Color Reversed
scene_description: In the chemical storage room, you examine the storage cabinets for poisonous substances (毒物). The cabinet is properly locked with restricted access, and the inventory log is current. However, the mandatory identification sign on the cabinet reads '医薬用外毒物' (Non-Pharmaceutical Poison) with red text on a white background. The facility's safety officer says the sign was recently replaced and 'matches the GHS color for acute toxicity.'
explanation: This is a non-compliance under Permits, Signage & Documentation. The Poisonous and Deleterious Substances Control Act requires poisonous substance (毒物) storage to display '医薬用外毒物' in white text on a red background (赤地に白文字). The sign shown has the colors reversed — red text on white background is the prescribed format for deleterious substances (劇物), not poisons. The GHS diamond color for acute toxicity is irrelevant; Japan's domestic signage requirements under this Act are separate from GHS implementation and must be followed exactly.
source_reference: Poisonous and Deleterious Substances Control Act Article 12, Enforcement Regulations Article 11-5, Article 11-6

title: SDS Available but Risk Assessment Not Updated for New Substances
scene_description: The chemical storage room has a binder of Safety Data Sheets organized alphabetically. You notice several recently procured chemicals — received within the last three months — have current SDS documents filed. However, when you ask to see the risk assessments for these new substances, the safety officer shows you a general risk assessment for the storage room that predates the procurement. None of the newly introduced substances have individual risk assessments.
explanation: This is a non-compliance under Chemical & Hazardous Materials. Since the 2024 ISHL amendment, risk assessment is mandatory for ALL substances that have GHS classification — the obligation now covers the full list of GHS-classified substances, not just the 674 substances on the notification list. New substances introduced to the workplace must have risk assessments completed before use. Having the SDS on file but failing to conduct the mandated risk assessment is a clear non-compliance with the expanded obligation under ISHL Article 57-3.
source_reference: ISHL Article 57-3 (2024 amendment), GHS Classification and Labelling Regulations

title: Autoclave Pressure Vessel Inspection Current
scene_description: In the instrument room, you check the autoclave — a pressure vessel used for sterilization. The inspection certificate is posted on the wall next to it, showing the most recent periodic self-inspection was conducted last month. The annual performance inspection by a registered agency was completed eight months ago. The daily pre-use check log is filled in consistently. The pressure relief valve has a current test tag.
explanation: This is compliant. Under the Ordinance on Boilers and Pressure Vessels (ボイラー及び圧力容器安全規則), pressure vessels require periodic self-inspections (monthly/annual by the employer) and performance inspections by a registered inspection agency. The autoclave meets all requirements: current agency inspection, regular self-inspections documented in the log, and a tested pressure relief valve. ISHL Article 38 sets the framework for these inspection obligations.
source_reference: ISHL Article 38, Ordinance on Boilers and Pressure Vessels Articles 67, 73, 88

title: Waste Manifest Missing for Specially Controlled Industrial Waste
scene_description: In the waste handling area, chemical waste drums are properly segregated and labeled. However, you ask to see the industrial waste manifests (マニフェスト) for the last shipment of specially controlled industrial waste — waste containing organic solvents above threshold concentrations. The waste handler produces receipts from the disposal contractor but cannot locate the E-manifest (electronic manifest) confirmations. He says 'the contractor handles all the paperwork on their end.'
explanation: This is a non-compliance under Chemical & Hazardous Materials. Under the Waste Management and Public Cleansing Act (廃棄物の処理及び清掃に関する法律), generators of specially controlled industrial waste must maintain their own manifest records — they cannot rely solely on the disposal contractor. The generator must confirm receipt by the transporter (B2 manifest) and final disposal confirmation (E manifest). Failure to maintain and confirm manifests at each stage is a violation of the generator's duty of care.
source_reference: Waste Management and Public Cleansing Act Article 12-3, Article 12-5, Manifest System Guidelines

title: PRTR Substance Tracking — Informal Records Only
scene_description: Still in the waste handling area, you ask about PRTR (Pollutant Release and Transfer Register) reporting. The facility uses several Class I designated chemical substances including chloroform and dichloromethane. The safety officer pulls up a spreadsheet showing purchase orders and estimated consumption — the totals suggest they're under the 1 ton/year threshold for most substances. However, the tracking is informal: it's based on procurement records rather than actual measured usage, and there's no formal procedure documented for annual PRTR threshold evaluation.
explanation: This situation is ambiguous. The PRTR Act requires facilities handling Class I designated chemical substances to determine whether they meet reporting thresholds. The facility does track quantities — via procurement records in a spreadsheet — but the method is informal and estimates rather than measures actual usage. An auditor could argue this is sufficient due diligence (the data exists and shows sub-threshold levels) or could cite the lack of a formalized procedure and measured usage data as a documentation gap. Best practice is to maintain a formal annual evaluation procedure, but the PRTR Act doesn't prescribe a specific tracking methodology.
source_reference: PRTR Act (Act on Confirmation of Release Amounts of Specific Chemical Substances) Article 5, Enforcement Order Article 5

title: Emergency Shower Flow Rate Below Standard
scene_description: In the emergency corridor, you test the emergency safety showers. The showers activate properly when the pull handle is used. However, when you time the flow, the water output appears notably weaker than expected. Checking the maintenance log, the last flow rate test recorded 50 liters per minute — below the JIS standard requirement. The maintenance technician noted 'flow reduced, scheduled for repair' three weeks ago, but no repair has been completed and the shower remains in service as the designated emergency unit.
explanation: This is a non-compliance under Emergency Preparedness. ISHL Article 24 and the Ordinance on Prevention of Hazards Due to Specified Chemical Substances require emergency washing facilities where workers handle hazardous chemicals. JIS T 7201 (aligned with ANSI Z358.1) specifies a minimum flow rate of 75.7 liters per minute for emergency showers. At 50 L/min, this shower fails the standard. More critically, a known deficiency was documented but left unrepaired for three weeks while the unit remains designated as the primary emergency shower — this represents a failure to maintain emergency equipment in operational condition.
source_reference: ISHL Article 24, Ordinance on Prevention of Hazards Due to Specified Chemical Substances Article 37, JIS T 7201
